critique.summary: The article critically examines the AI industry's claims about rapid data center expansion and GPU deployment, arguing that much of the announced capacity is speculative and not yet under construction. It highlights significant delays in data center building, power supply constraints, and questionable financial practices, while also discussing concerns about AI-driven software engineering practices in major tech companies.
mainPoints: Only about 33% of the announced 241GW of US data center capacity is under active development, with even less actually under construction.
Power supply and transmission capacity are major bottlenecks delaying data center completion and operation.
NVIDIA's GPU sales far outpace the rate at which data centers are being built and GPUs installed, leading to concerns about overinvestment and future supply gluts.
Several high-profile data center projects, including OpenAI and Oracle's Stargate Abilene, are significantly behind schedule.
There are allegations of GPU diversion to China involving Supermicro executives, raising questions about oversight and compliance.
AI-driven software engineering practices at hyperscalers are causing quality and security issues, with increased token usage incentivizing quantity over quality in code production.
The article suggests a broader industry and media coverup or misrepresentation of the true pace and scale of AI infrastructure development.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about data center construction delays and power constraints are supported by reports from credible industry analysts such as Wood Mackenzie, CBRE, and Sightline, which confirm slow build rates and power bottlenecks. The financial figures and timelines cited align with publicly available data on NVIDIA revenues and data center investments. The allegations regarding GPU diversion to China are based on a reported indictment and investigative journalism by Bloomberg and The Information, lending credibility to those claims. The discussion on AI software engineering challenges reflects documented incidents at companies like Meta and Amazon, corroborated by internal reports and reputable news sources. However, the article uses strong language and speculative assertions about conspiracies and coverups, which are less substantiated and should be treated cautiously. Overall, the factual claims are largely accurate but framed with a critical and somewhat sensational tone.
considerations: The article does not deeply explore the potential benefits and ongoing demand for AI compute that might justify some of the investments and buildout plans.
It largely focuses on US data centers and may not fully account for global developments or differences in other regions.
The complexity and variability of data center construction timelines and power infrastructure upgrades mean that delays are common but not necessarily indicative of fraud or collapse.
The article's critical tone and use of informal language may affect its perceived neutrality and could reflect the author's personal viewpoint.
The impact of future technological advances or policy changes on data center buildout and AI deployment is not discussed.
The article does not provide detailed perspectives from the companies or entities accused or criticized, limiting balance.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on skepticism about the AI industry's claims regarding data center power demands and financial viability. Commenters debate the realism of scaling AI infrastructure, the accuracy of industry projections, and the credibility of certain critics.

comments.author: jerf
comments.text:
I'm at a loss as to how some of these projects got funded in the first place. Anyone funding these should have had the perspective to see that there isn't enough power for them. Anyone funding them should have had the perspective to see that by the time power could come online for even a significant fraction of them, the depreciation and interest costs should have murdered the company trying to do it, especially if their solution to that problem is the oh-so-21st century solution of "solving" the problem of losing money by levering up. It does no good to go out of business entirely in 2027 to make the phat buxx in 2030, which seems to be the best case scenario for this space as a whole.

The other question I have is... who exactly is doing all of 1. Using AI right now 2. Making substantial money on it or getting real value and 3. Capacity constrained? Who is actually going to productively soak up all this capacity? It seems to me that bringing all this stuff online can't really make things much cheaper than they are now because the fixed costs aren't going anywhere, and if anything, trying to jam so many projects through all at once just raises those fixed costs even higher. It's not like they triple data center capacity (and increasing AI capacity by, what, 10x? 20x?), stick them full of AI systems, and into that 10x+ greater AI capacity they can sell it at the prices they are now. Higher capacity would crash the selling price but the costs would be as high or higher than now.

I am at a complete loss as to how the numbers are supposed to work here. You can't build a company in 2026 on the economy and tech infrastructure of 2036 anymore than it worked to build a company in 1999 on the economy and tech infrastructure of 2019, no matter how rosy the numbers look on the projections based on conveniently ignoring the fact the company passes through "death" in a year and half.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed financial and infrastructural critique of AI projects, highlighting realistic constraints on power and economic assumptions, making it a strong, well-reasoned contribution.

comments.author: motorpixel
comments.text:
Someone please correct my math.

The article says 240 Gigawatts of capacity is allocated for AI datacenters.

New York City draws about 10 Gigawatts in the hottest months of the year due to extra load from AC use.

So am I understanding correctly that these people want to foist upon the power grid 24 NYCs?
comments.analysis: This comment uses a relatable analogy to question the scale of AI power consumption claims, effectively grounding the discussion in understandable terms.

comments.author: wespiser_2018
comments.text:
Very good points.

My current model for understand for how AI will scale out is that we'll move through the following choke points:

AI chip makers -> Data center infra and construction -> regional power companies

Right now we're firmly in the "AI chip makers" part of the expansion, with everything else in the beginning stages. AI is useful, but whether it's hyped or not, it's hard to deny that not being able to build and power data centers will impact how this plays out.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a structured perspective on the stages of AI infrastructure scaling, adding depth to the conversation about practical limitations.

comments.author: awakeasleep
comments.text:
An analysis of datacenter commitments and GPU purchasing through how much power they will demand vs how much is available.

As someone who only has a passing interest, there isn't anything distilled enough in this article for me to comment on as the central point. Everyone seems to be reporting impossible numbers, and buying dramatically more hardware than they can install in a reasonable timeframe given the pace of the industry.
comments.analysis: This comment notes discrepancies between reported hardware acquisitions and realistic deployment timelines, supporting skepticism about industry claims.

comments.author: simianwords
comments.text:
I think the AI industry needs intelligent skeptics that keep the hype in check and ground us in reality.

But Ed Zitron is not it. Here's an example [1] of him fumbling on simple maths. He's also perpetually bearish without any sense of principles on his message.

This is what he wrote in 2024 [2]

> You can fight with me on semantics, on claiming valuations are high and how many users ChatGPT has, but look at the products and tell me any of this is really the future.

I think the industry really needs someone better with principles.

[1] https://x.com/binarybits/status/2034376359909130249

[2] https://www.wheresyoured.at/never-forget-what-theyve-done/
comments.analysis: While critical of a specific commentator, this comment calls for more principled skepticism, contributing constructively despite some emotional tone.

comments.author: 52-6F-62
comments.text:
Pointed and excellent.

But I wouldn't hold my breath waiting for introspection from that camp. It seems that AI maximalists, like so many other players these days, see it as end-game time. There are no bounds or rules: pick a side, and go. And then eat the rest.

Sure, not everyone sees it this way. There are highly competent, human actors working in their joy toward a better way forward with all of it. But I don't think you'll find that spirit unbridled inside any profit-seeking corporation of any significant standing (though I would be happy to be proven wrong). If it existed there, it is being choked out by selfishness and survivalism.

And then there's Thiel and ilk waxing eschatological, adding a whole other layer to the scheme.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a broader sociopolitical critique of the AI industry culture, which, while somewhat speculative, adds valuable context.

comments.author: redwood
comments.text: The article takes an odd turn in the second half and seems to veer from a very interesting deep-dive into how a lot of backlogged US data center production may correlate with GPU "slippage" via questionable resellers and GPU rental outfits to China
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a specific supply chain issue related to GPUs, adding nuance though less central to the main power and financial discussion.

comments.author: fred_is_fred
comments.text: Railroads, e-commerce, and AI - all useful, all were (or may be) credit/stock bubbles.  Railroads however have a much better depreciation schedule than GPUs.
comments.analysis: This historical analogy provides perspective on investment bubbles and asset depreciation, relevant but somewhat tangential.

comments.author: soumyaskartha
comments.text: The lying is not even subtle anymore. The gap between the demo and the product has never been wider and people are starting to notice.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration with perceived dishonesty in the AI industry, reflecting a common sentiment though lacking detailed evidence.

comments.author: babelfish
comments.text: I don't think Ed has made a single correct LLM prediction, despite posting in a fury probably monthly since ChatGPT was released. Grifters gonna grift
comments.analysis: This comment is dismissive and personal rather than analytical, reducing its constructive value and accuracy.

comments.author: 0gs
comments.text: i see him brag about how long these astroturf joints are on bluesky. had been a while since i'd tried to actually scroll through one and it makes sense now: so much more space to spawn subscription promos. better offline indeed
comments.analysis: This comment is off-topic and appears to be a disparaging remark unrelated to the main discussion, offering little insight.

comments.author: ramesh31
comments.text: Ah yes, now that the rails are all built what could we possibly do next?
comments.analysis: This short comment is vague and adds little to the conversation, lacking substantive content.

comments.author: consumer451
comments.text:
Something I heard a person say recently:

> Isn't it weird how there is no huge industry pushback on all this new AI datacenter power need, as there was about electrifying vehicles?
comments.analysis: This comment raises a valid point about the lack of public or industry resistance to AI power demands compared to other sectors, though it is more observational.
